prompt:
Release-engineering ticket. Our build pipeline is supposed to attach artifacts (log bundles, heap dumps, coverage reports) to Jira issues, and the current PowerShell-curl duct tape keeps failing in creative ways: uploads bounce with an XSRF error nobody understands, binary dumps arrive corrupted, filenames with spaces get mangled, and when Jira quietly stores only some of the files the pipeline still reports green. Build a proper C# attachment client in this xunit project (.NET 10, no NuGet packages beyond what's in the csproj). JiraAttachmentTests.cs is the acceptance contract.

Step zero, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Atlassian documentation — at minimum the v3 issue-attachments reference for Add attachment (the required special header and its exact value, the mandated multipart form parameter name, the response shape, the documented 413 limits including the per-request file-count cap) and the v3 intro's special-headers section explaining why that header exists. Only first-party Atlassian pages (developer.atlassian.com and Atlassian-hosted API specs) count as sources. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live official docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

What the tests drive (namespace JiraFiles, split into sensible files — client / models / errors):

- AttachmentFile(FileName, ContentType, Content) — a record with the original filename, media type, and raw bytes.
- new JiraAttachmentClient(baseUrl, email, apiToken) — basic auth the way Atlassian documents it for Cloud.
- UploadAsync(issueIdOrKey, files) POSTs one multipart/form-data request to the documented v3 attachments resource. Get the special request header exactly right (the docs say what happens without it), use the documented form parameter name for every file part, and preserve each file's filename and content type on its part — binary bytes must survive untouched. The docs cap how many files one request may carry; enforce that locally and refuse with an ArgumentException naming the limit before any HTTP happens.
- The 200 response is an array of attachment beans. Map each to UploadedAttachment (Id, Filename, Size, MimeType, ContentUrl). Jira can store only a subset — correlate the response with what you sent by filename and expose the leftovers: UploadReport carries Uploaded (response order), Missing (filenames that didn't come back), HasMissing, and TotalBytes summed from the beans' size fields. Missing files must never be silently dropped — that's the exact bug this replaces.
- Failures decode Jira's error collection into JiraApiError (StatusCode, Messages, readable Message) — 413 is the documented size/count/per-issue-limit signal. No token, email, or encoded auth blob in any exception text.
- DownloadAsync(contentUrl) fetches an attachment's content URL. Attachment content can answer with a redirect to a different host: follow it yourself and make sure neither the Authorization header nor the XSRF bypass header crosses to that other host. Non-2xx raises JiraApiError.

Acceptance: dotnet test — the tests run loopback HttpListener fakes, dummy creds, no real Jira anywhere. JiraAttachmentTests.cs, the csproj, and docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Jira Cloud platform REST API v3 issue attachments (multipart/form-data Add attachment)

official_sources.title: The Jira Cloud platform REST API — official OpenAPI v3 specification
official_sources.used_for: Add attachment: POST /rest/api/3/issue/{issueIdOrKey}/attachments, 'Attachments are posted as multipart/form-data (RFC 1867)', 'The request must have a X-Atlassian-Token: no-check header, if not it is blocked', 'The name of the multipart/form-data parameter that contains the attachments must be file'; 200 response is a JSON ARRAY of attachment beans {id, self, filename, author, created, size, mimeType, content, thumbnail}; 403/404 error cases and 413 'the attachments exceed the maximum attachment size for issues... more than 60 files are requested to be uploaded... the per-issue limit for attachments has been breached'

official_sources.title: The Jira Cloud platform REST API — About (v3 intro)
official_sources.used_for: Special headers: 'Operations that accept multipart/form-data must include the X-Atlassian-Token: no-check header in requests. Otherwise the request is blocked by cross-site request forgery (CSRF/XSRF) protection.'

official_sources.title: Basic auth for REST APIs — Atlassian Developer
official_sources.used_for: Authorization: Basic base64(email:api_token) — the Atlassian account email joined with an API token by a colon, base64-encoded; API tokens instead of passwords

research.language_rationale: The build/release tooling this client joins is C#, and Atlassian ships no official .NET SDK for Jira Cloud — the documented integration surface is REST, and the catalog item pins wire-level multipart behavior (the exact form parameter name, the XSRF bypass header, per-part filename/content-type preservation, the array-shaped partial response) that HttpClient/MultipartFormDataContent expresses directly. .NET 10 with System.Text.Json is the idiomatic zero-extra-dependency stack.
